title: Revelation 8
intro: Rev 8 is **the chapter where Bohr most decisively breaks from the pioneer Keith trumpets framework**. Smith reads the seventh seal (Rev 8:1) as the half-hour of silence in heaven at the second advent, and the first four trumpets (Rev 8:7-12) as the **Alexander-Keith identifications** drawn from Gibbon's _Decline and Fall_: **T1 = Alaric and the Visigoths (AD 395-410); T2 = Genseric and the Vandals' Mediterranean naval power (AD 428-468); T3 = Attila and the Huns (AD 433-453); T4 = Odoacer / Heruli + Ostrogothic / Justinian conclusion (AD 476-566)**. Bohr substitutes a **spiritual-historical / Christological framework**: **T1 = the fall of Jerusalem (AD 34-70); T2 = the fall of pagan Rome via the barbarian invasions (AD 378-476, with Visigoths and Vandals named); T3 = the rise of the papacy / great apostasy (Satan operating through his vice-gerent the Bishop of Rome); T4 = the papal 1260-year dominion (AD 538-1798), with sun=Christ, moon=Scriptures/JtB, stars=Reformers all partially eclipsed**. **Granularity of the divergence** — T1 (Jerusalem) and T4 (papal 1260) are the **deepest** breaks with Smith because they push entire trumpets into theological-spiritual territory that Smith assigns to seals and churches. T2 (fall of pagan Rome) is **largely convergent on the historical referent** (Bohr explicitly names Alaric 378/410 + Genseric 455 in his Summary, pp. 63-64); the divergence is in symbolic scaffolding (Bohr works from Jer 51:25 + Rev 17:15 + Babylon=Rome, not from Gibbon). T3 (rise of papacy) is **chronologically convergent** with Smith's Attila window (mid-5th c.) but historically divergent (different referent). The chief Miller-rule violations are **Rule XIV at T1 (the AD-95 Patmos vantage of Rev 1:1, 1:19 "things which shall be hereafter" rules out an AD-70 fulfillment), Rule V at T3/T4 (collapses trumpets into the same spiritual-eclipse domain already covered by the seals and the churches, producing doubling), Rule XII at T3/T4 (Bohr's sun/moon/star Christology is biblically rich but ignores the Joseph-dream and Dan-8:10 political-symbol pattern), and Rule XIII at T3/T4 (the "third-part" fraction lacks fulfilled specificity in Bohr's spiritualizing framework)**. The opening intercession scene (vv. 1-5) and the closing three-woes announcement (v.13) are convergent with Smith. Source: Bohr, _Revelation's Seven Trumpets: A Contextual Approach_ — Chapter 1 (Intro, pp. 3-24), Chapter 2 (T1 Jerusalem, pp. 25-36), Chapter 3 (T2 fall of pagan Rome, pp. 37-64), Chapter 4 (T3 great apostasy, pp. 65-93), Chapter 5 (T4 papal 1260, pp. 95-126), Three-woes textual location (pp. 141-147).

verses.text: And when he had opened the seventh seal, there was silence in heaven about the space of half an hour.

pioneerReading.html: **Seventh seal = the half-hour of silence in heaven at the second advent — the entire heavenly host (including the 24 elders, the seraphim, and the angelic ten-thousand-times-ten-thousand) accompany Christ to earth to gather the redeemed, leaving heaven temporarily empty.** "**The Seventh Seal. — This seal stands by itself. The first six brought us down to the second advent. The seventh introduces us to scenes connected with the second coming and the events immediately following. The half-hour of silence is the period when all heaven is empty, when the entire heavenly host accompanies Christ in his triumphant return to gather the redeemed. A prophetic half-hour, on the year-day basis, is about a week of literal time. The silence is not the silence of death but the silence of awe — the universe holds its breath as the great consummation arrives**" (DAR 452.2-453.1, condensed paraphrase). Smith cites EGW (_Early Writings_, _Great Controversy_) for the second-advent accompanying-host framework.

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, p. 13 (chart) + p. 325 (JFB quote in Chapter 14)
bohrReading.html: **Bohr's _Seven Trumpets_ does NOT give a dedicated exposition of Rev 8:1.** In the relationship-chart on p. 13 he pairs the 7th seal (silence in heaven) with the 7th trumpet (taking over the kingdom) as parallel consummations. The only substantive mention is in Chapter 14 (Trumpet 7), p. 325, where he approvingly cites Jamieson-Fausset-Brown: "the seventh trumpet, like the seventh seal and seventh vial, being the consummation, is accompanied differently from the preceding six: not the consequences on earth, but those IN HEAVEN, are described, the great voices and thanksgiving of the 24 elders in heaven, as the half-hour's silence in heaven at the seventh seal." Bohr's framework converges with Smith insofar as the seventh seal is read as a consummation-scene tied to the second advent, but he does not develop the angelic-host-accompanying-Christ exposition that Smith builds at DAR 452.4.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation. Convergent at the surface level (seventh seal = consummation tied to second-advent scene). Bohr does not develop a separate exposition of this verse in _Seven Trumpets_; Smith carries the angelic-host-accompanying-Christ exposition at DAR 452.4 without a Bohr counterpart.

verses.text: And I saw the seven angels which stood before God; and to them were given seven trumpets.

pioneerReading.html: **Seven angels = seven specific angelic agents (drawing on Luke 1:19 "_I am Gabriel, that stand in the presence of God_" as the standard pioneer identification of the "_standing before God_" class); seven trumpets = the seven successive prophetic-historical events / judgments of the trumpet series, paralleling the seven seals but covering the providential / political / military / judicial side of the same church-history span.** "**The trumpets, like the seals and the seven churches, embrace the period of the gospel dispensation from John's day to the end. The trumpets, however, are limited to events in the political / civil / military sphere — the providential judgments by which God dealt with the apostate church and the nations supporting her**" (DAR 453.2-454.1, condensed paraphrase). Smith's framework: the trumpets are **judgment-events** in **political history**, not in spiritual / theological history (which is the seals' and churches' domain).

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, pp. 3-24 (Chapter 1, Introductory Matters); summary chart p. 17
bohrReading.html: **Seven angels = the standard Luke-1:19 class of presence-angels; seven trumpets = the seven successive prophetic events.** Here Bohr's framework already begins to diverge from Smith. Bohr reads the trumpets not as Smith's **political-judgment events on the persecuting powers (Western Rome)** but as **a series of prophetic events spanning from Pentecost (AD 31, when Jesus began His intercession at the altar of incense) to the close of probation** that primarily address God's dealings with His own people and the apostate church (pp. 3, 13, 17, 18, 24). The chart on p. 17 maps T1 = Fall of Jerusalem (34-70), T2 = Fall of Rome (300-476), T3 = Church defiled (313-538), T4 = Papal dominion (538-1798), T5 = French Revolution (1789-1798), T6 = Gathering of righteous and wicked (1844-close of probation), T7 = close of probation / everlasting kingdom.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation at v.2 per se, but Bohr is already setting up the framework for the trumpet-1-through-4 divergence. The shift from "political-judgment events on persecuting powers" (Smith) to "prophetic events addressing God's people and the apostate church" (Bohr) prepares the spiritual / theological reading of trumpets 1-4 that follows.

verses.text: And another angel came and stood at the altar, having a golden censer; and there was given unto him much incense, that he should offer it with the prayers of all saints upon the golden altar which was before the throne.

pioneerReading.html: **The angel with the golden censer = Christ as our High-Priest-Intercessor, offering the prayers of all saints with the incense of his perfect-righteousness at the altar of incense (the first-apartment furniture of the heavenly sanctuary).** "**The angel here is none other than Christ our great High Priest, presenting before God in the heavenly sanctuary the prayers of his people, mingled with the merit of his own perfect righteousness (the much incense given unto him). The altar is the altar of incense, the first-apartment furniture. This is the intercessory-priesthood scene that frames the trumpet-judgments which follow**" (DAR 453.4-454.2, condensed paraphrase).

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, pp. 19-24 (Inauguration and Consummation: Two views of the censer; The Incense and the Censer)
bohrReading.html: **The angel = Christ as High Priest at the altar of incense, offering the prayers of all saints with the incense of his perfect-righteousness; convergent with Smith.** Bohr develops the intercessory framework extensively on pp. 19-24, with the censer / incense / altar-of-incense identification all convergent. Bohr adds the **Pentecost-as-start-of-trumpets** framing: "The starting point is the Day of Pentecost when Jesus began His intercession at the golden altar of incense in the holy place" (p. 3, restated p. 19). Bohr explicitly rejects the view that the censer-cast-down represents Pentecost: "on the Day of Pentecost Jesus began to offer the prayers of the saints mingled with the merits of His perfect life, He did not cast down the censer" (p. 21).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation on the censer-intercession identification. The Pentecost-as-start anchor is a Bohr framework-choice that begins to shape the trumpet-1 divergence (Bohr will read Trumpet 1 = Jerusalem AD 34-70, requiring the trumpet-series to start in the AD-30s; Keith / Smith have the trumpet-series start later, with Trumpet 1 = Alaric AD 395, since the trumpets are read as judgments on Western Rome and not on Jerusalem).

verses.text: And the smoke of the incense, which came with the prayers of the saints, ascended up before God out of the angel's hand.

pioneerReading.html: The smoke / incense rising = Christ's mediation effective before God; the prayers of the saints ascending. Smith treats vv. 3-4 as a single intercessory-scene block.

bohrReading.html: Same identification — Christ's mediation effective, prayers ascending; convergent with Smith. Bohr treats vv. 3-4 as a unit (the "first view of the altar: the censer intercedes", p. 20) and adds EGW supporting material on incense as the merits of Christ mingled with the prayers of the saints (pp. 22-24).

verses.text: And the angel took the censer, and filled it with fire of the altar, and cast it into the earth: and there were voices, and thunderings, and lightnings, and an earthquake.

pioneerReading.html: **The censer cast down with fire = the close-of-probation event, the cessation of intercession, after which the seven last plagues fall on the unrepentant (paralleled to Rev 11:19, Rev 16:18 — the voices/thunderings/lightnings/earthquake formula recurs at every climactic divine-judgment moment).** "**The casting down of the censer is the cessation of priestly intercession — the close of probation. The voices, thunderings, lightnings, and earthquake mark the great climactic divine-judgment event that follows the close of probation**" (DAR 453.4-454.2, paraphrase).

bohrReading.html: **The censer cast down = the close-of-probation event; convergent with Smith.** Bohr stresses the **end-of-series anchor** on pp. 20-21: "This introductory scene in Revelation 8 provides two points of reference: What Jesus is doing when the trumpets begin to sound and when they end... When the angel throws down the censer there is thunder, lightning, voices and an earthquake (Revelation 8:5). Revelation 15:5-8 describes the moment when the seventh trumpet is about to sound and the mystery of God finished (Revelation 10:7)." Bohr cross-links to Rev 11:19, 15:5-8, 16:17-21, and Ezekiel 10:1-2 (the man in linen scattering coals over the city). EGW citation on p. 24 from EW pp. 279-280 explicitly ties the censer-thrown-down to probation closing — convergent with Smith.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: No violation on the censer-cast-down close-of-probation identification. The Pentecost-to-close-of-probation framing is the **architectural** Bohr-choice that requires the trumpet-1-through-4 divergence to follow (since the trumpets must fit within this span, Bohr cannot use the late-Western-Rome dating Keith uses for Trumpet 1).

verses.text: And the seven angels which had the seven trumpets prepared themselves to sound.

pioneerReading.html: Brief transitional verse — the seven angels prepare to sound their trumpets in succession. Smith treats this as the formal introduction of the seven-trumpet sequence.

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, p. 25 (opening of Chapter 2, The First Trumpet)
bohrReading.html: Bohr does not give a separate exposition of v.6; the transition into the trumpet-sounding sequence is implicit in the opening of Chapter 2 (p. 25). Convergent at the formal level with Smith.

verses.text: The first angel sounded, and there followed hail and fire mingled with blood, and they were cast upon the earth: and the third part of trees was burnt up, and all green grass was burnt up.

pioneerReading.html: **Trumpet 1 = the invasion of the Western Roman Empire by Alaric and the Visigoths (AD 395-410), the first major judgment on Western Rome after its Christianization / Constantinian compromise.** "**The First Trumpet. — Alexander Keith, in his _Signs of the Times_, has clearly identified the historical fulfillment. The first trumpet was sounded when Alaric and the Visigoths swept down from the north and east upon the Western Roman Empire, beginning in A. D. 395. The hail represents the storm of barbaric invasion (cf. Isa 28:2 — 'a tempest of hail'); the fire mingled with blood, the destruction wrought by the invaders. The 'third part of the earth' refers to the Western division of the Roman Empire (the empire having been divided by Diocletian / Constantine into three primary prefectures, with the Western prefecture corresponding to the burnt third). The burning of trees and grass represents the destruction of inhabitants of all ranks — the trees (the great men, the nobility) and the grass (the common people). The campaign culminated in Alaric's sack of Rome in A. D. 410, the first time in 800 years that Rome had been taken by a foreign enemy. Gibbon, _Decline and Fall_, chapters 30-31, supplies the historical detail in extenso**" (DAR 455.5-457.3, condensed paraphrase; Smith quotes Keith and Gibbon at length).

bohrReading.html: **Trumpet 1 = the destruction of Jerusalem (AD 34-70) — the judgment on the Jewish nation for rejecting Christ and persecuting the early Christians.** Bohr's framework (paraphrased from pp. 25-36, with verbatim signals): "It is no coincidence that Ellen White begins the book The Great Controversy with a chapter on the destruction of Jerusalem because the sounding of the first trumpet refers to this event" (p. 25). "The churches and the seals begin in apostolic times and therefore the historicist method requires that the blowing of the trumpets begin also in apostolic times. Furthermore, the first trumpet must refer to a judgment that falls upon those who first oppressed God's people. What judgment fell upon oppressors of God's people in apostolic times? There is only one possibility—the destruction of Jerusalem and its temple" (p. 26). The hail-and-fire-mingled-with-blood echoes Ex 9:22-26 (Egyptian plagues on an oppressor of God's people) and Ezek 10:2 (man in linen with coals over the city, p. 26). The third-of-trees-burnt-up draws on Bohr's tree-symbolism (John the Baptist's fruitless tree, the cursed fig tree, the unfruitful vine — Israel as the dry tree, Luke 23:31; pp. 32-35); the grass = the common people (Isa 40:6-8 "all flesh is grass", pp. 35-36). Bohr abandons the Keith-Gibbon Alaric / Visigoths identification entirely; his framework is **spiritual-historical (judgment on the covenant-breaker nation for rejecting Christ)** rather than **political-military (barbarian invasion of Western Rome)**.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **Bohr breaks Miller Rules V, XII, XIII, and XIV.** **Rule V (Scripture as its own expositor):** the trumpet-series context (introduced as a sequence of judgment-events in vv. 2-6 following the seventh-seal silence of v.1, which Smith and Bohr both identify with the second advent — meaning the trumpets, as the unfolding of the seventh seal, span the gospel-dispensation up to the second advent) places the first trumpet **after** the AD-95 vantage of Rev 1:1, not before it. **Rule XII (trace figures through the Bible):** Bohr's hail-and-fire-mingled-with-blood identification (Ezek-9 + Ex-9 Egyptian-plague typology applied to Jerusalem) is Christologically suggestive but **does not match the political-empire-judgment pattern** of Daniel's prophetic stream (Dan 2 / 7 / 8 / 11 all read political symbols as political powers, not as spiritual/typological lessons). **Rule XIII (every word fulfilled):** Bohr's framework for v.7 — "the destruction of Jerusalem with hail-and-fire-mingled-with-blood as Ezekiel-9-style judgment" — has the **third-part-of-trees and all-green-grass details unattested in Josephus's account of the AD-70 siege**, while Keith / Smith's Alaric-Visigothic framework matches the **third-part-of-the-Western-Empire-prefecture geographical reach** and the **Gibbon-documented destruction of inhabitants** with verifiable historical specificity. **Rule XIV (must include actual fulfilled history):** **decisively — Trumpet 1 = AD 34-70 violates John's AD-95 vantage of Rev 1:1 / 1:19 ("things which must shortly come to pass" / "things which shall be HEREAFTER")**, since AD 70 was already 25 years past when John received the Apocalypse on Patmos. This is the **single most damaging Bohr-vs.-Miller violation in the audit so far**: Bohr's trumpet framework requires the prophet to be shown things already 25 years past as if they were still future, which directly violates the Rev-1:19 "_things which shall be hereafter_" framework.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Trumpet 1 (Smith: invasion of Western Roman Empire by Alaric and the Visigoths AD 395-410, with the hail-and-fire = barbarian-invasion storm and the third-part = Western prefecture; Bohr: destruction of Jerusalem AD 34-70, with the hail-and-fire = Ezek-9 / Ex-9 covenant-judgment typology and the third-part = leaders of Jerusalem). The hail-and-fire-mingled-with-blood symbol itself is redefined from Keith's "Isa-28:2 tempest of barbaric-invasion hail" to Bohr's "Ex-9 Egyptian-plague + Ezek-9 sealing-judgment hail."

verses.text: And the second angel sounded, and as it were a great mountain burning with fire was cast into the sea: and the third part of the sea became blood;

pioneerReading.html: **Trumpet 2 = Genseric and the Vandals' naval-power destruction of the Western Roman Mediterranean (AD 428-468), with the great mountain burning with fire = the Vandal kingdom established in North Africa under Genseric, and the third part of the sea = the Mediterranean (which the Vandals controlled with their fleets for nearly fifty years, until destroyed by the Eastern Empire's expedition of AD 468).** "**The Second Trumpet. — Keith identifies this with Genseric and the Vandal naval power. Genseric became king of the Vandals in A. D. 428; in 429 he crossed from Spain into Africa with eighty thousand men; in 439 he captured Carthage and established the Vandal kingdom of Africa; from this base his fleets ravaged the Mediterranean coasts of Spain, Italy, Sicily, Sardinia, Corsica, and Greece for nearly fifty years. In A. D. 455 Genseric sacked Rome itself, the second sack of the Eternal City. The great mountain burning with fire cast into the sea is an exact figure for this naval-power kingdom; the third part of the sea becoming blood matches the Mediterranean carnage. Gibbon supplies the documentation, _Decline and Fall_ chapter 33**" (DAR 457.4-460.1, condensed paraphrase).

bohrReading.html: **Trumpet 2 = the fall of pagan Rome via the barbarian invasions AD 378-476** — specifically the Visigothic incursion under Alaric (Roman army wiped out with Emperor Valens, 378 AD; sack of Rome 410 AD) and the Vandal sack under Genseric (455 AD), with the same Genseric-Vandal data Smith cites (pp. 63-64, Summary of the Second Trumpet). Bohr's symbolic chain is anchored in OT prophecy rather than Gibbon: the **great mountain burning with fire** = the destroying mountain of Jer 51:25 turned into a **"burnt mountain"** (pp. 41-42 — "this is the key verse to understand the second trumpet"); the **sea** = the multitude of nations that rose against the kingdom, per Jer 51:42 ("the sea has come up over Babylon") + Rev 17:15 ("the waters... are peoples, multitudes, nations") (pp. 42-43); **Babylon = Rome** by intertestamental usage (Sibylline Oracles, 2 Baruch, 1 Pet 5:13 "she who is in Babylon" written from Nero's Rome) (pp. 44-45). The mountain therefore is **the fourth head of Rev 17 = the Roman Empire** (pp. 44, head of Rev 17). The judgment-rationale is reciprocity (Gal 6:7): "the Roman Empire persecuted the Jews, and Christians such as Ignatius and Polycarp... therefore, when the second trumpet blew, God came in judgment against Rome" (p. 64). **Convergent with Smith on the historical referent (Visigoths, Vandals, sack of Rome 410 + 455); divergent on the typological scaffolding** (Smith works from Keith / Gibbon; Bohr works from Jer 50-51 + Rev 17 + the church-fathers "restrainer" tradition).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **No violation. Convergent at the level of fulfilled history; divergent on hermeneutic scaffolding.** Bohr identifies the **same barbarian-invasion period AD 378-476**, the **same Genseric / Vandal sack of 455**, and the **same prior Visigothic sack of Rome 410** that Smith / Keith cite — and even names Alaric's defeat of Emperor Valens at Adrianople (378) (p. 63). Bohr's symbolic justification (Jer 51:25 burnt mountain + Rev 17:15 sea-of-nations + Babylon=Rome) is internally consistent with **Rule V (Scripture interprets itself)** and **Rule XII (trace figures through the Bible)**, since each link is established by direct OT-NT cross-reference rather than typological speculation. The earlier audit-claim that Bohr lacks Vandal-naval-power specificity is **incorrect** — see pp. 63-64 where he explicitly names Genseric, the Vandals' 455 sack, and even the gold lampstand carried from Rome to Carthage. The genuine difference from Smith is **interpretive frame**, not **historical referent**.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Trumpet 2 (Smith: Vandals' naval power under Genseric AD 428-468, with great-mountain = Vandal kingdom in Africa and third-part-of-sea = Mediterranean trade lanes destroyed; Bohr: barbarian invasions of pagan Rome AD 378-476 including Visigoths under Alaric + Vandals under Genseric, with great-mountain = Roman Empire as the fourth head of Rev 17 / burnt mountain of Jer 51:25 and sea = multitude of nations rising against Rome per Jer 51:42 + Rev 17:15). **Historical referent largely overlaps; scaffolding differs (Smith = Keith/Gibbon political-military; Bohr = Jer 50-51 + Rev 17 OT/intertestamental).**

verses.text: And the third part of the creatures which were in the sea, and had life, died; and the third part of the ships were destroyed.

pioneerReading.html: **The destruction of ships and sea-creatures fits the Vandal naval-warfare specificity exactly** — the Vandals destroyed Roman merchant shipping wholesale, depopulated coastal regions, and the great Eastern-Empire expedition of AD 468 (1,113 ships, 100,000 men) sent against Genseric was itself destroyed in the harbor of Carthage by Vandal fire-ships, the third-part-of-ships destruction. The third-part-of-the-creatures-which-were-in-the-sea = the depopulation of the Mediterranean trade-and-fishing networks under Vandal raiding.

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, pp. 62-64 (The Fish, Ships, Summary of the Second Trumpet)
bohrReading.html: **Living creatures in the sea = the people / multitudes of the Roman Empire** (cross-linked via Hab 1:14-15 "You make men like fish of the sea", Ezek 29:3-4, Matt 4:19 "fishers of men", pp. 61-62); **ships = trading and commercial prosperity** of the Empire (Ezek 27:9, 25 the ships of Tarshish; Rev 18:17-19 the shipmasters lamenting Babylon's fall, p. 62). Bohr's Summary explicitly applies the v.9 imagery to the Roman context: "At the sounding of the second trumpet, instead of temporal prosperity, the barbarians invaded the Empire, destroyed the routes of commerce, and decimated the prosperity of Rome. The judgment of the second trumpet brought the collapse of the entire social and economic order of the Roman Empire... The barbarians destroyed the routes of commerce, decimated the cities and sacked Rome so that Rome became practically a ghost town" (pp. 63-64). Bohr does NOT cite the 1,113-ship Eastern Empire expedition of 468 AD that Smith / Keith / Gibbon highlight, but he does account for the third-part-of-ships symbolically as the destruction of Rome's commercial backbone by the barbarian invasions.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **No violation.** Bohr's symbol-chain (sea-creatures = people via Hab 1:14, ships = commerce via Ezek 27 / Rev 18:17-19) is internally consistent with **Rule XII (trace figures through the Bible)** and his historical application (collapse of Roman commerce under barbarian invasion) matches the Keith / Smith era. He does not pull out the specific Vandal-fire-ship destruction of the 468-AD Eastern Empire expedition that Smith dwells on, but this is a difference in **granularity of historical detail**, not a contradiction; both readings have the third-part-of-ships destroyed in the same period (late-5th-century Rome). The earlier audit's Rule-XIII flag is **withdrawn** — Bohr has explicit symbolic and historical accountability for the ships and the sea-creatures.

verses.text: And the third angel sounded, and there fell a great star from heaven, burning as it were a lamp, and it fell upon the third part of the rivers, and upon the fountains of waters;

pioneerReading.html: **Trumpet 3 = Attila and the Huns ("the Scourge of God"), the great star = Attila personally (the great barbarian leader whose meteoric career terrified the Western Empire AD 433-453); the rivers and fountains = the headwaters of the great Western European river systems (the Rhine, Rhone, Po, Danube) which Attila ravaged.** "**The Third Trumpet. — Keith identifies this with Attila and the Huns. Attila's career was as a star — sudden, meteoric, terrifying — and his self-designation 'the Scourge of God' (Flagellum Dei) fits the symbolism exactly. From his base in Pannonia he led the Huns westward, ravaging the river-fountains of central and western Europe — the Danube basin, the Rhine, the Rhone, the Po valley. The historical-specific detail of the third-part-of-the-rivers matches the geographical span of Attila's campaigns of AD 433-453**" (DAR 460.2-462.1, condensed paraphrase, citing Gibbon _Decline and Fall_ chapter 35).

bohrReading.html: **Trumpet 3 = the great apostasy of the church (rise of the papacy after the fall of the Roman Empire), with the falling star = Satan operating through his vice-gerent the Bishop of Rome.** Verbatim: "There can be little doubt that the 'falling star' of the third trumpet refers in a primary sense to Satan. Yet this star cannot refer to the original fall of Satan from heaven because that happened before creation week. Furthermore, it cannot refer to him falling from heaven at the cross... because the events of the third trumpet transpire well into the Christian era after the fall of Jerusalem (first trumpet) and the fall of the Roman Empire (second trumpet)" (pp. 65-66). Bohr's threefold justification for star = Satan-through-papacy: (1) "what the Bible attributes to Satan he actually accomplishes through human apostate powers" (Rev 12:4 dragon-through-Herod; GC p. 438 "the dragon, primarily, represents Satan, [and] is, in a secondary sense, a symbol of pagan Rome"; p. 66); (2) Dan 7 / Rev 13 persecution = work of the little-horn-papacy for 1260 days, paralleled by Rev 12:6, 13-14 same 1260 days as work of the dragon — "thus Satan ruled during the 1260 years through his emissary, the Bishop of Rome" (p. 67); (3) 2 Thess 2:3-4 man of sin exalts himself like Satan in Isa 14:12-14, and is energized by Satan per 2 Thess 2:9 (p. 67). The **rivers and fountains** = the pure waters of the gospel / Word of God flowing through the church (Ps 1:3, Prov 13:14 "the law of the wise is a fountain of life", John 4:13-14 / 7:37-39 living water / Holy Spirit), pp. 81-83. The **falling star pollutes the waters** = the papacy corrupts the doctrine of the church (pp. 87-92, citing GC, EW, DA: "the priests and rulers, the scribes and Pharisees, destroyed the living pastures, and defiled the wellsprings of the water of life", DA 478; "the Christian religion became corrupted and the church lost its purity and power", EW 211). Bohr places this period **after the fall of pagan Rome and before the fourth trumpet of the 1260 years**, i.e., the transition period from the fall of the Western Empire (~476) to the establishment of papal supremacy (538) — chronologically convergent with Smith's Trumpet 3 window. **Bohr abandons the Keith-Attila identification** and substitutes the rise-of-papacy / great-apostasy framework.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **Bohr breaks Miller Rules V, XII, XIII, and XIV.** **Rule V (Scripture as its own expositor):** the trumpet-series in vv. 2-6 is introduced as a sequence of **divine judgments** in the political-military domain (paralleling the seals' spiritual-judgment domain — the two series are complementary, not redundant). Bohr's reading collapses Trumpet 3 into the same domain already covered by Seal 3 (the black horse = era of compromise) and Seal 4 (the pale horse = the papal millennium of death), creating a **redundancy** Smith's framework avoids. **Rule XII (trace figures through the Bible):** Bohr does have legitimate cross-link support for star = Satan (Isa 14:12, Rev 12:7-9, Luke 10:18) and for waters = doctrine / Holy Spirit (John 4:13-14, 7:37-39), but the **political-historicist** stream (Dan 8:10 stars = earthly leaders cast down; Dan 2 / 7 / 8 / 11 political symbols = political powers) supports Keith's reading of star = Attila as a great barbarian leader who fell upon the rivers of central Europe. **Rule XIII (every word fulfilled):** Bohr cannot tie the third-part-of-rivers-and-fountains detail to specific datable events; he treats the third-part fraction as a generic intensifier rather than a fulfilled geographic measure (whereas Keith / Smith point to Attila's campaigns across the upper-Danube / upper-Rhine / Rhone / Po basins as the literal third-part). **Rule XIV (must include actual fulfilled history):** Bohr's framework leaves the trumpet-3 vacancy that Smith fills with the documented Attila campaigns AD 433-453. **Caveat:** Bohr's chronological placement (transition from fall of Rome to rise of papacy) does overlap Smith's window (Attila c. 451 falls within the same era), so the *time* is not in dispute — only the *historical referent*.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Trumpet 3 (Smith: Attila and the Huns AD 433-453, with great star = Attila personally and rivers = central-European river basins he ravaged; Bohr: rise of papacy / great apostasy, with great star = Satan operating through the Bishop of Rome as his vice-gerent, and rivers / fountains = pure waters of the Word / Holy Spirit flowing through the church now polluted by papal doctrine).

verses.text: And the name of the star is called Wormwood: and the third part of the waters became wormwood; and many men died of the waters, because they were made bitter.

pioneerReading.html: **The name "Wormwood" = the bitter and deadly consequences of Attila's invasion (Wormwood = absinthe, the bitter plant; the figure of bitter waters carrying death matches the bitter-and-deadly-consequence character of the Hunnic invasions). The many men dying of the waters = the great mortality wrought by the Hunnic campaigns across central-and-western Europe.** Smith stresses that the bitterness is **figurative for the bitter consequences** of the Hunnic invasions, not a literal poisoning of literal waters.

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, pp. 85-92 (Wormwood and Apostasy; The Defiled Waters; Edwin Thiele and Ellen White; Jon Paulien Summarizes the Third Trumpet)
bohrReading.html: **Wormwood = false doctrine introduced by the papal apostasy; the bitter waters = the corrupted gospel; many men dying = spiritual death of those who drink the papal teaching.** Bohr's biblical chain for wormwood-as-apostasy: Deut 29:17-18 (turning from God breeds "a root bearing bitterness or wormwood"); Amos 5:7, 6:12 (forsaking righteousness = wormwood); Jer 23:15 ("thus says the LORD of hosts concerning the prophets: 'Behold, I will feed them with wormwood, and make them drink the water of gall'"); Jer 9:13-15 (when Israel forsook the law, "I will feed them with wormwood and give them water of gall to drink"); Prov 5:3-5 (the immoral woman's end is bitter as wormwood) — applied to the apostate church via the Rev 2 Jezebel / Rev 17 harlot link (pp. 85-86). Bohr cites Edwin Thiele directly: "The once pure, life-giving fountains become contaminated and corrupt as the death-star Wormwood falls upon them, and henceforth men die rather than live as they partake of the polluted waters... Satan rather than Christ is in control" (Thiele, _Outline Studies in Revelation_, pp. 293-294, quoted p. 89). EGW chain (pp. 89-90): GC 567 (degrading priestly confession defiles), DA 478 ("the priests and rulers... defiled the wellsprings of the water of life"), EW 211 (heathen-Christian merger corrupted the church). Jon Paulien quoted on "three objects of judgment in the third trumpet: the star, the springs and rivers, and the people who drink the water" (p. 92).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **Same Rule V / XII / XIII / XIV violations as v.10.** Bohr's wormwood-as-papal-apostasy reading has strong OT cross-link support (Jer 9:15, 23:15, Amos 5:7, Deut 29:18), but the trumpet-series context still anchors the verse in political-historical judgment-events on the persecuting powers, and Keith's bitter-consequences-of-Attila's-invasions reading retains the better historical specificity for "many men died of the waters" (the documented mortality of the Hunnic campaigns).

verses.text: And the fourth angel sounded, and the third part of the sun was smitten, and the third part of the moon, and the third part of the stars; so as the third part of them was darkened, and the day shone not for a third part of it, and the night likewise.

pioneerReading.html: **Trumpet 4 = Odoacer and the Heruli (AD 476) + the Ostrogothic and Justinian conclusion (AD 476-566), with the abolition of the Western Roman Empire and the consequent darkening of the political "luminaries" (sun = emperors, moon = senate, stars = consuls and lesser magistrates).** "**The Fourth Trumpet. — Keith identifies this with the events that completed the abolition of the Western Roman Empire. Odoacer the Herulian deposed Romulus Augustulus, the last Western Emperor, in A. D. 476. The Western Empire's institutions — the imperial office (the sun), the senate (the moon), the consular and magisterial offices (the stars) — were progressively extinguished. The Ostrogothic kingdom under Theodoric (AD 493) continued the eclipse; the Justinian wars of reconquest (AD 533-554) and the final extinction of the senate (AD 566) closed the period. The third-part-darkening matches the geographical-political third-part-of-the-Western-Empire framework**" (DAR 463.1-467.1, condensed paraphrase; Smith cites Gibbon _Decline and Fall_ chapters 36-45).

bohrReading.html: **Trumpet 4 = the papal 1260-year supremacy AD 538-1798 — the partial eclipse of Christ (sun), the Scriptures / John the Baptist (moon = lesser light), and the witness of the faithful (stars = Reformers, prophets, teachers).** Bohr's identifications: **Sun = Christ, the Sun of Righteousness** (Mal 4:2; Ps 27:1 "the LORD is my light"; Ps 84:11 "the LORD God is a sun"; John 8:12 / 9:5 "I am the light of the world"; Rev 1:16 face shining as the sun), p. 96-98. **Moon = lesser light** — John the Baptist ("He was the lesser light, which was to be followed by a greater", DA 220, quoted p. 99) + the Old Testament / Scriptures as testimony to Christ (John 5:35-47, Luke 24:25-27, 44-46; Isa 8:20; Ps 119:105), pp. 99-101. **Stars = teachers / messengers / Reformers** — Rev 1:20 (stars = angels of the churches = ministers), Rev 12:1 (twelve stars = twelve apostles), Dan 12:3 ("those who are wise shall shine like the brightness of the firmament"), Dan 8:10 (little horn cast down stars = killed those who taught righteousness during the 1260 years); Bohr explicitly names Wycliffe as the "morning star of the Reformation" (p. 104). **The 1260-year eclipse** = the two witnesses prophesying in sackcloth (Rev 11:3, GC 267, GC 269), pp. 105-106. Bohr explicitly chains: "The fourth trumpet darkness was a precursor to the fifth trumpet where the darkness became even more intense to the point of a total eclipse of the light of the two witnesses in the French Revolution" (p. 106). Bohr abandons the Keith-Odoacer / Heruli / Ostrogoth / Justinian political-eclipse framework and substitutes the Christological-sun + lesser-light-Bible + Reformer-stars framework spanning the papal 1260 years.

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **Bohr breaks Miller Rules V, XII, XIII, and XIV.** **Rule V:** the trumpet-series is introduced as a sequence of *political-military judgment-events* (parallel-not-redundant to the seven seals); Bohr's reading collapses Trumpet 4 into the same spiritual-eclipse domain already covered by the **fourth seal** (pale horse = Death and Hades = papal millennium of persecution) and by the **church of Thyatira** (Jezebel-papacy). **Rule XII:** Bohr's cross-link justification for sun=Christ / moon=lesser-light / stars=Reformers is biblically rich and rigorously developed (he supplies 15+ verses for each identification), but the **Joseph-dream pattern** (Gen 37:9-10 sun/moon/stars = political-family-rulers) and the **Dan 8:10 little-horn-casting-down-stars** pattern support the political-historicist reading of sun/moon/stars as imperial/senatorial/magisterial offices. **Rule XIII:** the "third-part-darkened" detail and "a third of the day did not shine, and likewise the night" prophetic-specifics fit Keith's geographical-Western-third reading with documented specificity (Odoacer 476 + Ostrogoth fall 553 + final Justinianic extinction of the senate 566), while Bohr's "1260-year partial eclipse" cannot account for the precise *third-part* fraction. **Rule XIV:** Bohr's framework **doubles** with Trumpet 3 (both = papal apostasy / suppression of Word) and with the seals (Seal 3 black horse + Seal 4 pale horse), leaving the trumpets-as-distinct-series without distinctive historicist content. Keith's framework gives each of the four trumpets a separate documented-historical referent (Visigoths / Vandals / Huns / Heruli-Ostrogoths-Justinian) with no doubling. **Note:** This is a *frame* violation, not a *factual* misrepresentation — Bohr's data are tightly OT-NT-EGW-sourced; the violation is in choosing the wrong frame for the trumpet-series.

symbols.label: Symbols redefined
symbols.html: Trumpet 4 (Smith: abolition of Western Roman Empire under Odoacer and the Heruli AD 476 + Ostrogothic / Justinian conclusion to AD 566, with sun = imperial office, moon = senate, stars = consular / magisterial offices; Bohr: papal 1260-year dominion AD 538-1798, with sun = Christ the Sun of Righteousness, moon = lesser-light Scriptures / John the Baptist / Old Testament, stars = apostles / teachers / Reformers — Wycliffe explicitly named as morning star of the Reformation).

verses.text: And I beheld, and heard an angel flying through the midst of heaven, saying with a loud voice, Woe, woe, woe, to the inhabiters of the earth by reason of the other voices of the trumpet of the three angels, which are yet to sound!

pioneerReading.html: **The three woes = the fifth (Saracen), sixth (Ottoman / Turkish), and seventh (final / second-advent) trumpets — distinguished from the first four political-judgment trumpets on the Western Empire by their geographical focus (Eastern Empire) and their religious-military character (Mohammedan / Ottoman / final-consummation).** "**The angel's announcement marks the transition from the four trumpets that brought down the Western Empire to the three woes that would afflict the Eastern Empire and bring the world to its final crisis. The fifth woe is the Saracen-Mohammedan invasion that hammered the Eastern Empire from the 7th century onward; the sixth woe is the Ottoman / Turkish power that destroyed the Eastern Empire in 1453; the seventh woe is the final consummation. The 'inhabiters of the earth' phrase is the same Rev-13/14 technical phrase for the unrepentant**" (DAR 468.1-469.1, condensed paraphrase).

bohrReading.citation: _Seven Trumpets_, pp. 141-147 (Textual Location of the Three Woes; Perspective of Revelation 12, 13, 14)
bohrReading.html: **Bohr's three-woes mapping: First woe = fifth trumpet = the French Revolution 1789-1798 (Rev 11:7-10 killing of the two witnesses + Rev 13:9 deadly wound to papacy via the sword 1793-1798); Second woe = sixth trumpet = 1798 → close of probation (Rev 11:11-14 resurrection of the two witnesses, Advent awakening, three angels' messages polarizing the world; the gathering of the wicked enemies and the remnant); Third woe = seventh trumpet = Christ takes over the kingdoms at His coming, finishing the mystery of God (Rev 10:7).** Verbatim: "Revelation 8:13: When the fourth trumpet has blown, there is an announcement of three woes to come. Revelation 9:1-11: The first woe are the events of the fifth trumpet... Revelation 9:12: 'One woe is past'... Revelation 11:14: After the resurrection of the Bible and the mention of the enemies and the remnant, the text tells us that the second woe is past: 'The second woe is past. Behold, the third woe is coming quickly'... The sixth trumpet carries us from 1844 all the way to the close of probation when the mystery of God is finished (Revelation 10:7). This occurs when the seventh trumpet is about to sound. When the seventh trumpet sounds, Jesus takes over the kingdoms of the world at His coming. The blast of the seventh trumpet is the third woe" (pp. 142-144). **Bohr does NOT use the Saracen / Ottoman identifications for trumpets 5 and 6** (that historicist mapping is in Smith / Keith); his Rev-9 framework is the French-Revolution-and-end-time identification (treated in detail in the Rev-9 audit).

status.label: Miller-rule status
status.html: **No violation at v.13 itself.** The verse only announces three woes; it does not identify them. Both Smith and Bohr agree that the three woes = trumpets 5, 6, 7, that they fall on "the inhabiters of the earth" (Rev 13/14 technical phrase for the unrepentant), and that the seventh trumpet is the consummation tied to Christ's coming. The substantive divergences are in Rev 9 (Smith: Saracens / Ottomans; Bohr: French Revolution + 1844-to-close-of-probation) and Rev 11 (Smith: seventh-trumpet sounding 1844; Bohr: seventh-trumpet sounding at the second advent). The earlier audit's claim that Bohr reads the sixth woe as Armageddon-gathering and the seventh woe as merely close-of-probation was **factually wrong** — Bohr's chart explicitly assigns the seventh trumpet to Jesus taking the kingdoms at His coming, which is what Smith holds as well.

summaryHtml:
**Rev 8 violation summary:** Of 13 verses, Bohr has **Miller-rule violations at vv. 7, 10, 11, and 12** (Trumpets 1, 3, and 4); Trumpet 2 (vv. 8-9) was found on closer reading to be **largely convergent with Smith on the historical referent** (Bohr explicitly names Alaric / Visigoths 378 and 410, Genseric / Vandals 455, in his Summary at pp. 63-64) — divergent only in symbolic scaffolding (Bohr works from Jer 51:25 burnt-mountain + Rev 17:15 sea-of-nations + Babylon=Rome via 1 Pet 5:13, rather than from Gibbon). The genuine violations cluster at T1 (Jerusalem AD 34-70 violates John's AD-95 Patmos vantage of Rev 1:1, 1:19 — **Rule XIV**) and at T3-T4 (rise-of-papacy + papal-1260 collapses the trumpet series into the same spiritual-eclipse domain already covered by Seal 3, Seal 4, and the church of Thyatira — **Rule V doubling**; sun/moon/stars treated as Christological symbols rather than political-Joseph-dream symbols — **Rule XII**; the third-part fraction loses fulfilled specificity in the spiritualizing frame — **Rule XIII**). The opening intercession scene (vv. 1-5), v.6 transition, and v.13 three-woes announcement are convergent with Smith. **Two factual misrepresentations were found in the prior extracted text and corrected**: (1) the prior v.13 Bohr-reading claimed Bohr identifies the sixth woe as Armageddon-gathering and the seventh woe as merely close-of-probation — Bohr's actual chart (pp. 142-144) assigns the sixth trumpet to 1798-to-close-of-probation (encompassing 1844 + the three-angels' messages + the polarization of the world) and the seventh trumpet to Jesus taking over the kingdoms at His coming, which is what Smith holds as well; (2) the prior T2 reading claimed Bohr has no Vandal-Genseric specificity — Bohr's pp. 63-64 Summary explicitly cites both. **Citations corrected**: all six Bohr citations in vv. 7-13 were realigned from inflated page ranges (e.g., pp. 100-130 for v.7, pp. 131-180 for vv. 8-9, etc.) to the actual chapter ranges in _Seven Trumpets_ (pp. 25-36, 37-64, 62-64, 65-93, 85-92, 95-126, 141-147). The pre-edit citations appear to have been generated by line-number-as-page extraction or arbitrary scaling.
